summaries.Economics: The main elements of the FSM economy vary from state to state and include subsistence farming, local fishing, lease of fishing rights to FSM waters, servicing of fishing fleets, government employment, and tourism. Tourism is considered to have potential for near-term income generation due to the natural and marine beauty of the islands, though limitations in air transportation, land-use issues, and competition with other island destinations closer to tourist markets represent challenges to developing larger-scale tourism investment. The FSM considers the ocean to be the country's most important resource, earning $18-24 million annually in recent years through licensing fees paid by foreign vessels for tuna fishing in the FSM's Exclusive Economic Zone. Rising sea levels are identified as a threat to infrastructure and local economies, exacerbating existing inequities in the Pacific Islands region, with livelihoods and economy specifically cited as areas of concern under this threat. Socioeconomic data gaps are also noted as a challenge across Pacific Island jurisdictions, perpetuating historical social injustices reinforced by colonial and postcolonial governance systems.
summaries.Time zone: Howland Island and Baker Island observe UTC-12:00, designated as Nautical Letter Y (Yankee), making them part of the United States Minor Outlying Islands in that offset zone. This places them in the IDLW (International Date Line West) time zone, which is the Western Hemisphere side of the International Date Line. As a result, Howland and Baker Islands represent the latest place on Earth where any given calendar date remains valid, making them the reference point for the "Anywhere on Earth" (AoE) calendar designation, which is used to specify deadlines without requiring timezone calculations by anchoring to the last location on Earth where a date is still current.
summaries.History: In 1791, Edmund Fanning, an American sailor, discovered Howland Island, marking its entrance into recorded history. The first recorded sighting of the island in 1822 was by George Worth, a Nantucket whaling-ship captain, who named it Worth Island. In 1842 another American whaling ship visited the island, and it was renamed Howland Island for that ship's lookout. The United States claimed Howland in 1857, along with nearby Baker Island, under the Guano Act of 1856. Prior to 1890, organic nitrate (guano) was mined from the island by both the United States and the British, though the atoll's guano deposits are now exhausted. In 1937, famous pilot Amelia Earhart attempted to land there during her flight around the world but never made it, adding mystery to the island's story. The island was colonized from Hawaii in 1935, and during World War II it became a lookout point for the U.S. military. Today it is a nature reserve and part of the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge system, with very few people visiting and keeping its history alive.
summaries.National Wildlife Refuge: Howland Island, a coral atoll and unincorporated territory of the United States located in the southwestern Pacific Ocean approximately 1,650 miles southwest of Honolulu, is designated as a U.S. National Wildlife Refuge and is currently part of the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge system, providing nesting areas and forage for a variety of birds and marine wildlife, including several species of migratory seabirds and shorebirds as well as threatened and endangered sea turtles. The island was also designated part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument in 2009, which helps safeguard marine resources in the area. The island has no permanent inhabitants and serves an essential role for science and ecology, with researchers visiting to study its wildlife and environment, contributing to the protection of coral reefs and unique bird species. The island is composed of coral fragments and is surrounded by an active fringing reef, and high-resolution satellite images taken by astronauts aboard the International Space Station are used to update geographic maps of reefs, assess the health of reef ecosystems, and calculate bathymetry of the surrounding ocean bottom.
summaries.Earhart Light: The Earhart Light is a small lighthouse located on Howland Island, built in 1937 in anticipation of Amelia Earhart's scheduled refueling stop on the island during her transpacific flight attempt on July 2, 1937. Although her radio broadcasts were heard on Howland, her plane never arrived, and it is thought she most likely crashed in the waters just north of Howland Island. The lighthouse was rendered useless when a Japanese submarine fired 50 shells into the area during World War II following a bombing raid by 14 Japanese planes on Howland Island in 1941. The station has been inactive since 1942 and is now considered a "day beacon." Brief visits have been made to the island to restore the light beacon and to add the names of two colonists killed during the Japanese attack. The beacon, which holds the memory of Earhart's intended voyage, continues to stand on Howland Island, which is now a National Wildlife Refuge under the jurisdiction of the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service.
summaries.Image gallery: The gallery of Howland Island includes a map of Howland Island and a digital image photographed by an Expedition 10 crewmember aboard the International Space Station (ISS), captured on 3 December 2004, with the image identifier ISS010-E-09287. The image was taken using a Kodak DCS760c Electronic Still Camera with an 800 mm focal length, from an altitude of 192 nautical miles (356 km), at a look angle of 41 degrees in the SW direction, with a sun azimuth of 115° and sun elevation of 19°, and a cloud percentage of 10%. The image source is the JSC Gateway to Astronaut Photography of Earth, authored by the Earth Science and Remote Sensing Unit at Lyndon B. Johnson Space Center. The photograph shows Howland Island, a United States possession in the north Pacific, surrounded by an active fringing reef visible through white breakers encircling the island, and was taken as part of a global reef mapping and monitoring program conducted by astronauts aboard the Space Station, with high-resolution images used to update geographic maps, assess reef ecosystem health, and calculate bathymetry of the surrounding ocean bottom.
